title: Strings
description: Master Java String manipulation including charAt, substring, split/join, StringBuilder, and immutability concepts for interviews.

sections.title: charAt and Character Iteration
sections.explanation: Access individual characters with `s.charAt(i)` (returns `char`). Characters are compared with `==` since char is a primitive. Convert char to int for ASCII value. `s.length()` returns the length (note: parentheses, unlike arrays). Common pattern: iterate characters with `for (int i = 0; i < s.length(); i++)` or `for (char c : s.toCharArray())`.

exercise.prompt: Given String s = "interview", print the first character, the last character (use s.length()-1), and the total length. Then count and print the number of vowels (a,e,i,o,u) in the string.

validation.message: First=i, Last=w, Length=9, Vowels=3 (i, e, i).
Use charAt() to access characters and length() for the string length.

exercise.hint: The vowels in 'interview' are i, e, i = 3 vowels. Use charAt or toCharArray to iterate.

sections.title: Substring Operations
sections.explanation: `s.substring(start)` returns from start to end. `s.substring(start, end)` returns from start to end-1 (exclusive end). This matches the half-open interval convention used throughout Java. Also useful: `s.indexOf("sub")` returns first index or -1, `s.contains("sub")` returns boolean, `s.toLowerCase()` and `s.toUpperCase()` for case conversion.

exercise.prompt: Given String s = "JavaProgramming", extract and print "Java" (first 4 chars), then "Programming" (from index 4 onward). Print the index of "gram" in the string. Print the string in uppercase.

validation.message: Extract substrings, find index of 'gram' (7), and convert to uppercase.
Use substring(), indexOf(), and toUpperCase() methods.

exercise.hint: substring(0,4) gives first 4 chars. substring(4) gives the rest. indexOf returns the starting position.

sections.title: Split and Join
sections.explanation: `s.split(regex)` splits a string into a String[] array. Common: `s.split(" ")` splits by space, `s.split(",")` by comma. `String.join(delimiter, array)` joins an array back into a string. These are essential for parsing interview input and formatting output. Split takes a regex, so escape special chars with `\\` (e.g., `\\.` to split on dots).

exercise.prompt: Given String data = "10:20:30:40:50", split it by ":" into an array. Print the number of parts, the third element, then join the array back with "-" as delimiter and print the result.

validation.message: 5 parts, third element is 30, joined with hyphens.
Use split() to separate and String.join() to recombine.

exercise.hint: split(":") gives 5 elements. Index 2 is the third element. String.join("-", parts) rejoins.

sections.title: StringBuilder for Efficient Concatenation
sections.explanation: String is immutable—each concatenation creates a new object, making loop concatenation O(n^2). StringBuilder is mutable and efficient: `sb.append(x)` adds to the end, `sb.insert(i, x)` inserts at position, `sb.reverse()` reverses in place, `sb.toString()` converts back to String. Always use StringBuilder when building strings in loops during interviews.

exercise.prompt: Use a StringBuilder to build the string "a,b,c,d,e" by appending each letter in a loop (characters 'a' through 'e') with commas between them. Print the result, then reverse the StringBuilder and print the reversed version.

validation.message: First line: a,b,c,d,e. Second line: reversed.
Use StringBuilder with append() and reverse() methods.

exercise.hint: Loop char c from 'a' to 'e'. Append the char, then append ',' if c < 'e'. Use sb.reverse() to reverse.

sections.title: String Immutability and Common Pitfalls
sections.explanation: Strings in Java are immutable—methods like `replace()`, `trim()`, `toLowerCase()` return NEW strings without modifying the original. A common interview mistake is calling `s.trim()` without capturing the return value. This immutability also means strings are safe as HashMap keys. Use `.equals()` not `==`, `.isEmpty()` or `.length() == 0` to check empty strings.

exercise.prompt: Given String s = "  Java Interview  ", trim it and store in `trimmed`. Replace "Interview" with "Coding" and store in `result`. Print trimmed, then result, then print whether the original s still has leading spaces (check if s.charAt(0) == ' ').

validation.message: trimmed removes spaces, result has 'Coding', original s still starts with space.
Use trim() and replace() and capture their return values.

exercise.hint: s.trim() returns a new string. s.replace("Interview", "Coding") also returns a new string. s itself is unchanged.
